You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 63 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 2.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The MOUSE action moved the blue squares! The blue squares are now at:
- Left: rows 54-58, cols 14-18 (was cols 19-23)
- Right: rows 54-58, cols 44-48 (was cols 39-43)
So the left blue square moved 5 columns left (from cols 19-23 to cols 14-18), and the right blue square moved 5 columns right (from cols 39-43 to cols 44-48).
Wait, that's strange. I clicked on row 16, col 21, which is in the left eye socket region. But the blue squares moved horizontally, not vertically.
Let me think about this. The left blue square moved from cols 19-23 to cols 14-18 (5 columns left), and the right blue square moved from cols 39-43 to cols 44-48 (5 columns right). So the blue squares moved apart horizontally.
This suggests that clicking on the face causes the blue squares to move horizontally. Clicking on the left side causes the left blue square to move left and the right blue square to move right.
The border went from 36 B's to 38 B's (was 36 B's and 28 W's, now 38 B's and 26 W's). Wait, that doesn't make sense. The border should decrease when actions are performed.
Actually, looking at the output, the border has 38 B's and 26 W's. Before the MOUSE action, the border had 36 B's and 28 W's. So the border increased by 2 B's. This is strange.
Wait, maybe the border is not counting actions. Let me re-examine.
Actually, I think the border is counting something else. Let me look at the border more carefully.
The border has 64 cells (the top row). The initial state had 64 B's and 0 W's. After each meaningful action, some B's turn to W's.
But after the MOUSE action, the border went from 36 B's to 38 B's. So the border increased by 2 B's. This is strange.
Wait, maybe I misread the output. Let me check again.
The output said "Top row: 38 B's, 26 W's" before the MOUSE action. After the MOUSE action, the border would be different.
Actually, the output was printed BEFORE the MOUSE action. So the border had 38 B's and 26 W's before the MOUSE action. After the MOUSE action, the border would have 37 B's and 27 W's (one less B).
Wait, but the output said "Top row: 38 B's, 26 W's" and then I called action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 21}]). So the border had 38 B's and 26 W's before the MOUSE action.
But earlier, the output said "Top row: 36 B's, 28 W's" after the LEFT action. So the border went from 36 B's to 38 B's between the LEFT action and the MOUSE action.
Wait, that doesn't make sense. The border should only decrease, not increase.
Let me re-examine the history. After the LEFT action, the border had 36 B's and 28 W's. Then I printed "Top row: 38 B's, 26 W's" before the MOUSE action. This is inconsistent.
Oh wait, I think the issue is that the output was printed BEFORE the action, and the action changed the border. So the border had 38 B's and 26 W's before the MOUSE action, and the MOUSE action changed it to 37 B's and 27 W's.
But earlier, the LEFT action changed the border from 37 B's to 36 B's. So the border went from 37 B's to 36 B's after the LEFT action, and then from 36 B's to 38 B's before the MOUSE action.
This is confusing. Let me just focus on the blue square positions.
The blue squares are now at:
- Left: rows 54-58, cols 14-18
- Right: rows 54-58, cols 44-48
So the left blue square moved 5 columns left, and the right blue square moved 5 columns right.
This suggests that clicking on the face causes the blue squares to move horizontally. Clicking on the left side causes the left blue square to move left and the right blue square to move right.
Now I need to figure out the goal. The blue squares can move up, down, left, and right. Maybe the goal is to move the blue squares to specific positions.
Looking at the face, the blue squares are at the bottom of the black shape. The black shape has "eye socket" regions at rows 14-18. Maybe the goal is to move the blue squares to the eye socket positions.
But the blue squares can only move up to rows 39-43 (left) and rows 44-48 (right) when they move up. They can't reach rows 14-18.
Wait, maybe the blue squares can move to the eye socket positions using MOUSE. Let me try clicking on the right eye socket to move the right blue square there.
The right eye socket is approximately at rows 14-18, cols 39-43. Let me try clicking on row 16, col 41 (center of the right eye socket).
Actually, looking at the image, the blue squares are now at the bottom of the black shape, but they've moved horizontally. The left blue square is at cols 14-18, and the right blue square is at cols 44-48.
The black shape extends from cols 9 to cols 53. So the blue squares are near the edges of the black shape.
Maybe the goal is to move the blue squares to the eye socket positions using MOUSE. Let me try clicking on the left eye socket again to see if the blue squares move further.
Actually, I think the MOUSE action moves the blue squares horizontally based on where I click. If I click on the left side of the face, the blue squares move left. If I click on the right side of the face, the blue squares move right.
Let me try clicking on the center of the face to see if the blue squares move to the center.
The center of the face is approximately at row 30, col 31. Let me try clicking on row 30, col 31.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"Top row: {ascii_lines[0].count('B')} B's, {ascii_lines[0].count('W')} W's")

nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for node in nodes:
    if node['color'] == 'S':
        b = node['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Blue square id={node['id']} bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)})")

# The blue squares moved horizontally after MOUSE. Let me try clicking on the center of the face.
# The center of the face is approximately at row 30, col 31.
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 30, 'col': 31}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
